schema.howto_name: How to Tell If Amethyst Is Real

howto_steps.name: Look for color zoning
howto_steps.text: Genuine amethyst shows natural lighter/darker purple gradients, concentrated at tips. Synthetic is uniform.

howto_steps.name: Check inclusions under a 10x loupe
howto_steps.text: Natural amethyst has needle rutile and two-phase inclusions; synthetic has 'breadcrumb' bubbles.

howto_steps.name: UV fluorescence check
howto_steps.text: Synthetic amethyst often fluoresces blue under shortwave UV; natural is usually inert.

howto_steps.name: Temperature and hardness for bracelets
howto_steps.text: Genuine amethyst feels cool and (on rough stones only) scratches glass.

howto_steps.name: Cross-check price and naming
howto_steps.text: Large 'natural amethyst' geodes at tumbled-stone prices are a red flag.

compliance.gentle_note: Amethyst identification tests combine practical mineralogy (color zoning, inclusions, fluorescence) with marketplace awareness. Home tests help you spot common fakes, but for valuable amethyst geodes, a gemological lab report is the gold standard. Crystal meanings and energetic properties are separate from authenticity.
compliance.brand_tagline: We name our stones and sources — return to what's real.
